other_observers: bird was seen before, I believe, by Mark Peterson; Not sure who found the birds this year or whether they were here in 2004. In 2003 they were found here by Tony Leukering and apparently stayed to breed.
specific_location: NW corner of intersection of Huerfano CR 310 and the railroad tracks, approximately half a mile west of exit 42 on I-25 south of Walsenburg
description: The bird was a brick-red tanager, without strong markings anywhere. The bill was dark and shorter than I'd expect on Summer Tanager; the cheek area had a sooty wash on top of the red color. Size and shape were similar to Western Tanagers in the same area.Behaviors: The bird was singing from the tops of junipers, especially dead parts of live junipers, on a slope well uphill from the road. It was only occasionally singing from places where it could be seen. I thought at certain points that there could have been two males singing, but I was not able to confirm this, and the presence of singing male Black-headed Grosbeaks could be a spoiler on that point.Call: The bird was located by its song, which was surprisingly similar to that of Black-headed Grosbeak, and rather unlike that of other Piranga tanagers. The presence of singing Black-headed Grosbeaks on the same hillside complicated ID, at least until I was able to get a visual on the singing male HepTan.Plumage: adult alternate
